FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study (5.1 release), 21 U.S. recruitment sites, baseline N~12,000 aged 9-10. Methods: 'The current study used data from the baseline (Y0: N=11,876; M_age=9.9), the 2-year follow-up (Y2: N=100,972; M_age=12.0), and the 4-year follow-up (Y4: N=4754; M_age=14.1).'

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: parallel-process latent growth curve models testing bidirectional longitudinal associations between social media hours and cognitive performance trajectories (crystallized, Flanker inhibitory control/attention, Pattern Comparison processing speed) across 4 years.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Research on Adolescence (2025); no indication of being a duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Social media hours measured as self-reported daily hours visiting social networking sites (Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, etc.). Table 3 (Model 5) reports slope-slope path s_sm -> s_cryst: beta = -.22, p < .001, p-adj < .001, pairing social media hours trajectory with crystallized ability trajectory (individual-trait cognitive outcome). Also Model 6: s_flanker <-> s_sm: beta = -.09, p = .031; Model 7: s_pattern <-> s_sm: beta = .11, p = .001.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple numeric estimates reported: beta coefficients, SEs, p-values, 95% CIs in Tables 2 and 3 for all parallel-process models.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis (latent growth curve modeling) linking social media hours to individual-trait cognitive outcomes with quantitative results throughout.

FT_EC7_reason: Social media is IV in multiple analyses (social media slope predicting crystallized, Flanker, and Pattern Comparison slopes). EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis; that condition is not met here.
notes: Longitudinal parallel-process LGC models across 3 time points (Y0, Y2, Y4) of ABCD 5.1. Social media hours as both IV and DV in bidirectional intercept-slope associations. Clear INCLUDE.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study (release 4.0, N=8,699) alongside Generation R (N=3,739). ABCD-specific estimates are reported separately throughout (Table 2 and Table 3 have dedicated ABCD columns with separate estimates and CIs).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: target trial emulation (TTE) and G-formula applied to ABCD and GenR longitudinal data to estimate prospective associations and counterfactual effects of screen time (and other modifiable factors) on internalizing symptoms.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology (2025); no evidence of duplication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable estimate found anywhere in main tables, supplementary, or sensitivity results. The exposure is 'recreational screen time (ST)' measured as overall daily hours from parent report. Step 2: The composite is not exclusively phone/SM items. The paper explicitly states 'we used an overall recreational ST metric' (Discussion, p. 263) that is undifferentiated by device or modality. No breakdown by cellphone, TV, gaming, or social media is provided. The composite bundles all recreational screen activities without isolating phone/SM use. NOT_MET under Step 2 (non-phone/non-SM activities not separately broken out).

notes: Screen time variable is undifferentiated recreational ST (hours/day, all screens combined), not specifically phone or social media. No per-modality breakout exists anywhere in the paper. Excluded under FT-EC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study (5.0 release), 'multisite, longitudinal study with 11,868 participants enrolled at baseline from 21 U.S. sites,' baseline ages 9-10 years. Final analytic sample N=11,819.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: random-intercepts cross-lagged panel models (RI-CLPMs) across 4 waves of ABCD data examining bidirectional longitudinal associations between OSA time and ADHD problems.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in European Child & Adolescent Psychiatry (2025); no evidence of duplication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 2 (no single platform estimate found at Step 1; OSA is a composite). Composite content per Methods: 'we summed the time spent on "texting", "visiting social networking sites" and "video chatting" into a combined category of OSA.' All three items are exclusively phone/SM/digital-socializing activities. No TV, console gaming, PC gaming, or other non-phone ingredients included. Other side = ADHD problems (CBCL attention problems T-scores), an individual-trait psychiatric/behavioral outcome. MET under Step 2.

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric estimates reported throughout: standardized beta coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values from RI-CLPMs in Figure 1, Table 2 (bivariate correlations), and Table 3 (cross-lagged estimates by sex). E.g., OSA T2->ADHD T3 for females: beta=0.094 (SE=0.041), 95% CI [0.013, 0.175], p=0.023.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential RI-CLPM analyses linking OSA time (phone/SM composite) to ADHD problems (individual-trait outcome) with quantitative results reported.

FT_EC7_reason: Bidirectional RI-CLPM design: OSA time appears as both IV (predicting ADHD) and DV (ADHD predicting OSA time) across waves. EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis with no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere; that condition is not met.
notes: OSA composite = texting + social networking sites + video chatting (all phone/SM/digital-socializing). Bidirectional RI-CLPM with 4 waves. ADHD problems as outcome is individual-trait. Clear INCLUDE.
